title: Hormuz Traffic Picks Up, Russia Considers Diesel Export Ban, and the Nuclear Inspection Dispute Clouds Iran Deal
subhead: Ship transits through Hormuz are climbing toward prewar levels, but the central route remains mined and Iran denies it agreed to nuclear inspections. Russia is weighing a full diesel export ban as Ukrainian drone strikes cripple its refineries, and Venezuela is courting oil majors for a post-Maduro rebuild.

sections.heading: Hormuz Traffic Recovers, but Central Route Remains Mined
sections.body:
Ship transits through the Strait of Hormuz are accelerating. Kpler tracking confirmed 131 ships crossed between Friday and Monday, including 39 on Monday alone — still below the prewar daily average of 100-130, but a substantial improvement over the near-zero traffic during the blockade. India's MEA spokesperson Randhir Jaiswal said 11 India-bound vessels have transited since the MoU, with 10 Indian-flagged ships still in the Gulf. An International Maritime Organization evacuation plan for 11,000+ stranded seafarers is now underway with safety guarantees from Iran, Oman, and the U.S., according to IMO Secretary-General Arsenio Dominguez.

But recovery is uneven. The main central route through Hormuz remains mined and closed, according to Kpler. Ships are using the smaller northern route through Iranian waters and the southern route through Omani waters. Iran's lead negotiator Mohammad Bagher Qalibaf told state media that Iran would manage the strait in accordance with international maritime law and said he was heading to Oman to discuss Hormuz governance. Under the framework agreement, Iran has 30 days to demine the central channel and must allow toll-free transit for 60 days. Bank of America's Francisco Blanch told El Pais he expects 40% traffic restoration by end of July, 75% by end of September, and full normalization by year-end. Citi's base case (60% probability) projects oil markets moving into surplus, with prices trending to $60-65 per barrel by Q1 2027.
sections.whyItMatters: The gap between Trump declaring Hormuz 'totally open' and the reality of a mined central channel encapsulates the deal's fragility. Transit numbers are improving, but the 60-day toll moratorium and demining deadline mean the strait's governance will be contested territory for months. If Iran charges tolls after 60 days, or if demining drags, the recovery stalls.

sections.heading: Nuclear Inspections Dispute Undermines Iran Deal Credibility
sections.body:
The first round of U.S.-Iran talks in Switzerland concluded on June 22 with a roadmap for a final deal within 60 days. Pakistan and Qatar are mediating. Four working groups were established: Sanctions Termination, Nuclear Affairs, Reconstruction and Economic Development, and Monitoring/Implementation, according to Iran's IRNA citing Deputy Foreign Minister Kazem Gharibabadi.

But a sharp disagreement has already emerged on nuclear inspections. U.S. Vice President JD Vance said Iran had agreed to allow international nuclear inspectors back into the country, calling it 'a big deal.' Iran's Foreign Ministry spokesperson Esmail Baghaei flatly contradicted this on Tuesday, telling reporters that IAEA inspectors were not scheduled to examine bombed enrichment sites. Trump posted that Iran had agreed to inspections 'into infinity' and that without this concession 'there would be no further negotiations.' Iran's Geneva ambassador Ali Bahreini pushed back further, saying 'Iran is the only country who decides what to do with those assets' regarding unfrozen funds. The IAEA has not confirmed any inspection agreement.

Separately, the fate of Iran's estimated $6 billion in unfrozen assets is also disputed. Vance said unfrozen funds would be used to buy American-grown corn, wheat, and soy, with U.S. and Qatari oversight. Iran's foreign ministry called that claim baseless, saying import decisions would be based on 'prices and quality.' Iranian President Pezeshkian visited Islamabad on Tuesday for talks with Pakistani leaders, his first trip abroad since the war began.
sections.whyItMatters: The public disagreement on inspections is the first serious crack in the MoU's veneer. If Iran refuses IAEA access to bombed nuclear sites, the entire monitoring framework collapses — and with it, congressional support for any deal. The unfrozen-assets dispute signals that even 'agreed' provisions mean different things to each side.

sections.heading: Russia Considers Diesel Export Ban as Ukrainian Strikes Cripple Refineries
sections.body:
Russian Deputy Prime Minister Alexander Novak announced on Tuesday that Russia is considering a complete ban on diesel exports, a dramatic escalation from current restrictions that only cover non-producers. Russia has already banned gasoline and jet fuel exports for all market participants. Speaking at a televised government meeting chaired by Putin, Novak said oil companies had delayed refinery maintenance and were tapping reserves to meet domestic demand.

The fuel crisis is spreading. Russia's gasoline output last week was down approximately 25% from the June 2025 daily average, according to industry sources cited by Reuters. Seaborne oil product exports fell roughly 15% in the first half of June compared to the first half of May. Fuel rationing is now in effect in more than 50 Russian regions, including Siberian cities like Omsk and Irkutsk — thousands of kilometers from the front. Vedomosti reports the government is also considering importing fuel and subsidizing it to cap domestic prices.

In Crimea, the situation is tighter still. Sevastopol's Russian-installed governor Mikhail Razvozhayev announced enforced restrictions on public life: public transport closes at 10 p.m., large shops and cafes at 8 p.m., street lighting dimmed, and mass outdoor activities banned. Putin, in his first public comments on the Ukrainian strikes, claimed they had 'no effect' on the war — at a meeting called specifically to address the fuel crisis those strikes helped create.
sections.whyItMatters: The world's third-largest crude producer considering importing fuel and banning diesel exports is a direct measure of Ukraine's drone campaign's success. A full diesel export ban would tighten global product markets, hitting buyers like Brazil and Turkey that still rely on Russian diesel. The fuel crisis reaching Siberia shows this is no longer a border-region problem — it is a nationwide supply failure.

sections.heading: China Chokes Mineral Exports to Japan as Xi Pressures Takaichi
sections.body:
China has nearly halted exports of some forms of tungsten to Japan, with shipments falling to zero in January and staying there through 2026. Exports of dysprosium and terbium — rare earths critical for EV magnets — have also been at zero for all of 2026, the last shipment to Japan having been in October 2025, just before PM Sanae Takaichi's remarks about Taiwan that angered Beijing. Yttrium exports to Japan are at 1.13% of 2025 total volume. The curbs fall short of a blanket ban — some refined metal and light rare earths continue to flow — but they are clearly calibrated to inflict targeted pain without triggering a coordinated Western response.

The impact is tangible. Sumitomo Electric CEO Masayoshi Matsumoto told Bloomberg in Beijing that 'if we're shut out by China, it's definitely going to cause problems for Japanese manufacturing.' Mitsubishi Materials now relies on recycled material for 70% of its feedstock, targeting 100% by 2030. Japan's ambassador to China has repeatedly requested meetings with Chinese Foreign Ministry officials without success in 2026. Takaichi reportedly does not want to meet Xi at APEC in November.

At the G7 summit last week, leaders pledged to reduce reliance on any single country for rare earths and permanent magnets to less than 60% by 2030, with an ultimate goal of 50%. Japan has joined the pledge. Japanese Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama said recycling initiatives, price floors, and trade measures are all under consideration.
sections.whyItMatters: Beijing is weaponizing mineral supply chains against Japan with surgical precision — enough to hurt Japanese auto and defense manufacturers, not enough to force a coordinated G7 retaliation. The timing, tied to Takaichi's Taiwan comments, signals that mineral exports are now conditional on political alignment. Japan's recycling push and the G7 diversification pledge are responses, but they operate on a 5-year timeline that China is exploiting now.

sections.heading: Energy Fuels Buys Germany's VAC in $1.9B Non-China Magnet Play
sections.body:
U.S.-based Energy Fuels announced a $1.9 billion cash-and-stock deal to acquire Germany's Vacuumschmelze (VAC), a century-old magnet manufacturer with 3,600 employees and more than 1,000 customers including General Motors. Energy Fuels will pay private equity firm Ara Partners $718 million in cash plus 65.85 million newly issued shares. Ara will hold roughly 20% of the combined company and get a board seat.

The deal directly targets the China dependency problem. Energy Fuels CEO Ross Bhappu told Reuters that building a magnet business from scratch is difficult and that VAC's existing customer relationships were key: 'It also takes a long time to get customer acceptance of those magnet products. VAC has that customer acceptance.' VAC operates plants in Germany, the U.S., Malaysia, and China, with expansions planned for a South Carolina facility that opened late last year. The deal is expected to close early next year.

The acquisition aligns with the G7 critical minerals alliance pledge to reduce single-supplier dependency to below 60% by 2030. Combined with MP Materials and USA Rare Earth's own magnet ventures, the deal marks the most significant Western industrial response yet to China's export controls on rare earths.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the first major M&A deal that directly connects Western rare earth mining to an established magnet manufacturer. It short-circuits the 5-7 year timeline for building non-China supply chains by buying an existing business rather than building one. Whether it works depends on whether VAC's China plant stays open — and whether Energy Fuels can supply enough feedstock to replace Chinese sources.

sections.heading: Oil Prices Near Four-Month Lows as Markets Bet on Hormuz Recovery
sections.body:
Brent crude fell below $80 per barrel — more than $40 off its war peak of $126 — and extended losses on Wednesday June 24, trading near four-month lows on expectations of smoother crude flows through Hormuz. U.S. WTI traded around $73. Average U.S. gasoline prices fell below $4 per gallon, according to AAA. Bank of America revised its 2026 Brent forecast down to an average of $82 per barrel, from an earlier estimate of $92.50, and projects prices in the $70-80 range in the second half.

The IEA's monthly oil market report, released June 17, projected a significant supply surplus in 2027 as Hormuz flows recover and shut-in production restarts. Iraq said its oilfields are ready to resume production and restore previous rates. Approximately 11 million barrels per day of Middle East production was shut in during the Hormuz closure, according to IER analysis, and restarting that capacity will take months. About 500 ships, including 220 oil tankers, remain parked in the Persian Gulf waiting to transit, according to Politico. The U.S. is working with insurers to restore coverage for Hormuz transits.

But the downward trajectory faces risks. Iran briefly reclosed the strait on Saturday over Israel's Lebanon strikes. The toll question remains unresolved. And the IEA warns that global strategic petroleum reserves are at their lowest levels in decades, requiring replenishment that will add demand pressure.
sections.whyItMatters: The market is pricing a full Hormuz recovery and a 2027 supply glut. That's the base case — but it assumes the 60-day negotiating window holds, demining finishes on schedule, and Iran doesn't impose tolls. Any of those assumptions breaking would reverse the price decline rapidly. The SPR replenishment requirement also means demand won't fall as fast as supply rises.

sections.heading: Asia's Energy Emergency Forces Accelerated Diversification
sections.body:
The Iran war has forced a wholesale rethink of energy security across Asia, according to a CFR analysis published Monday. The Asian Development Bank forecasts a 0.7 percentage point drop in GDP growth for the region in 2026 and inflation rising to 5.2% if oil averages $96 per barrel. The Philippines declared a national energy emergency in March after local diesel and petrol prices more than doubled. Bangladesh purchased LNG spot cargo at nearly three times normal benchmark prices. Vietnam, Thailand, South Korea, and Bangladesh imposed energy rationing; Indonesia and Malaysia ordered work-from-home mandates; Pakistan and the Philippines shifted to four-day work weeks.

The response has been swift. Chinese solar exports to the Philippines roughly tripled in Q1 2026 year-on-year, making it one of the fastest-growing solar markets in the region. Indonesia accelerated its 100 GW solar target. Vietnam expanded EV incentives and charging infrastructure. Five ASEAN members — Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines, Thailand, and Vietnam — are now actively pursuing nuclear power, with Malaysia setting a 2031 target for its first reactor. Vietnam advanced a nuclear cooperation deal with Russia this week. Several Southeast Asian governments, including Indonesia, the Philippines, and Vietnam, secured Russian crude as Gulf supplies dried up, and looked to U.S. and Australian LNG as longer-term alternatives.

The IEA's Southeast Asia Energy Outlook 2026 projects renewable capacity could nearly triple by 2035 under current policies or grow fivefold if newly announced targets are met. Japan, which sources 95% of its oil through Hormuz-dependent routes, remains the most exposed major Asian economy — and now faces China's mineral export throttling on top of the energy shock.
sections.whyItMatters: The Iran war is doing what decades of climate diplomacy couldn't: forcing rapid energy diversification across Asia. The irony is that Chinese solar panels and Russian crude are filling the gap left by Middle East supply disruption, creating new dependencies even as old ones are broken. The nuclear push across ASEAN — long resisted on safety and cost grounds — is the most striking structural shift.

bottomLine: Hormuz is reopening in practice if not in principle: 131 ships crossed in four days, an IMO evacuation plan for 11,000 stranded seafarers is underway, and oil prices are near four-month lows. But the Iran deal's first week has already exposed a nuclear inspections dispute that calls the entire framework into question, the central channel remains mined, and the toll regime is merely deferred, not resolved. Meanwhile, Russia is considering banning all diesel exports as Ukrainian drone strikes cut gasoline output by 25% and fuel rationing spreads to Siberia. China is surgically choking rare earth and tungsten exports to Japan. And across Asia, the energy shock is accelerating diversification — toward solar, nuclear, and Russian crude — at a speed no policy document ever achieved. The common thread: every major energy development this week involves a state weaponizing or rethinking a supply chain it previously took for granted.
sources: AP News, Reuters, Bloomberg, El Pais, NPR, CBS News, Oilprice.com, CFR, The Straits Times, CNBC, Firstpost, Kyiv Independent, IER, Kpler, IEA, AAA, Politico, Energy Fuels press release, Vedomosti, Euromaidan Press, Japan Times, Invezz
